coredata.dc:title: Designed p-type graphene quantum dots to heal interface charge transfer in Sn-Pb perovskite solar cells

coredata.dc:description: In Sn-Pb based low bandgap perovskite solar cells (LB-PSCs), the large energy loss of ~0.5 eV lags its development, originating from unfavorable band alignment at the interface and serious charge recombination in the device. Here, judiciously designed graphene quantum dots by functional N/Cl doping are applied at PEDOT:PSS/perovskite interface to modulate the energy level and charge transfer simultaneously. Among three designed graphene quantum dots, the N, Cl codoped one (N,Cl-GQDs) induces the best band alignment, because of the enhanced p-doping. Besides, N,Cl-GQDs best regulate the charge distribution and passivate the defect states at the interface by π-conjugated effect and electronegative Cl, paving the way for efficient interface charge transport. Upon N,Cl-GQDs optimization, the FA0.6MA0.4Sn0.6Pb0.4I3 based PSCs achieves impressive efficiency of 21.5% (1 sun, AM1.5) with a high photovoltage of ~0.89 V (energy loss of ~0.36 eV) and fill factor over 80%, which are among the best parameters in reported LB-PSCs. The N,Cl-GQDs modified device exhibits the best shelf stability after 1000 h, due to the least trap states and reformative interface. This work highlights designed graphene quantum dots for photovoltaic applications.

(a) Schematic diagram of the quantum dots deposition; (b) AFM topographical images for PEDOT:PSS with and without (w/o) modification; (c) SKPM images for PEDOT:PSS w/o modification; (d) Schematic energy levels of each layer in PSC. head.ce:title: Designed p-type graphene quantum dots to heal interface charge transfer in Sn-Pb perovskite solar cells

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In Sn-Pb based low bandgap perovskite solar cells (LB-PSCs), the large energy loss of ~0.5 eV lags its development, originating from unfavorable band alignment at the interface and serious charge recombination in the device. Here, judiciously designed graphene quantum dots by functional N/Cl doping are applied at PEDOT:PSS/perovskite interface to modulate the energy level and charge transfer simultaneously. Among three designed graphene quantum dots, the N, Cl codoped one (N,Cl-GQDs) induces the best band alignment, because of the enhanced p-doping. Besides, N,Cl-GQDs best regulate the charge distribution and passivate the defect states at the interface by π-conjugated effect and electronegative Cl, paving the way for efficient interface charge transport. Upon N,Cl-GQDs optimization, the FA0.6MA0.4Sn0.6Pb0.4I3 based PSCs achieves impressive efficiency of 21.5% (1 sun, AM1.5) with a high photovoltage of ~0.89 V (energy loss of ~0.36 eV) and fill factor over 80%, which are among the best parameters in reported LB-PSCs. The N,Cl-GQDs modified device exhibits the best shelf stability after 1000 h, due to the least trap states and reformative interface. This work highlights designed graphene quantum dots for photovoltaic applications.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Upon N,Cl-GQDs optimization, the FA0.6MA0.4Sn0.6Pb0.4I3 based PSCs achieves impressive efficiency of 21.5% (1 sun, AM1.5) with a high photovoltage of ~0.89 V (energy loss of ~0.36 eV) and fill factor over 80%.

ce:sections.ce:para: Perovskite solar cells are an up-and-coming next generation photovoltaic technology because of high power conversion efficiency (PCE) and low-cost fabrication [1–3]. Recently, the narrow bandgap (<1.3 eV) tin-lead (Sn-Pb) mixed PSCs are getting attention with the additional benefit of utilizing in tandem solar cell technology [4–7].

Although various reports have demonstrated that the PCE of Sn-Pb PSCs is more than 20%[4,8], which is still lower than that of Pb-only PSCs, especially in terms of open-circuit voltage loss [9]. Unsatisfied open-circuit voltage (V oc) < 0.8 V of the Sn-Pb PSCs remain a lingering cloud lagging their development [10–12]. Energy loss (Eg-qV oc) as large as ~0.5 eV firstly originates from the unfavorable band alignment at the interfaces of the PSCs [13,14]. The energy level of widely used PEDOT:PSS as hole transport layer (HTL) does not match perfectly with the valence band position of the Sn–Pb perovskites, causing potential energy loss at the PEDOT:PSS/perovskite interface and diminution of the V OC of the device [10,15]. Secondly, V oc is seriously reduced by the non-radiative recombination at the trap states in the device [16–19]. Trap sites usually stem from facile point defect formation in the perovskite crystals and uncoordinated bonding at the film surface [20]. Sn based perovskite is more susceptible to defect formation than pure Pb perovskite due to the energetically unfavorable s-p antibonding between Sn-5 s and I-5p. Thus, Sn and I vacancies are easily formed by the break of Sn-I bond and oxidation of Sn2+ [21,22]. Besides, the weak bonding nature of organic-inorganic perovskite makes the defect situation more serious. These trap states, either deep or shallow, severely reduce the carrier lifetime and output of the devices. By using GuaSCN to passivate the defect states, the energy loss of the as fabricated PSC is reduced to 0.42 eV with PCE more than 20% [12]; surface-anchoring zwitterionic molecules as reducing agent in precursor suppress the trap states and reduce the energy loss to 0.37 eV with PCE of 21.7% [8]. State-of-the-art Voc vs Eg of recently reported low-bandgap Sn–Pb mixed PSCs is summarized in Fig. S1.

Interface engineering has been successfully applied in the context of Sn-Pb PSCs specifically at the HTL/PVK interface, reducing the interface energy mismatch and passivating the interface defect states [8,10,23–26]. Thereby, the V oc can be enhanced. In contrast to the insulating modifier at the interface, semiconducting molecules for modification usually exhibit prominent functions of efficient charge transport, along with energy band alignment and defects diminution, guaranteeing improved V oc and fill factor (FF) at the same time [27,28]. Among functional semiconductors for interface modification, the graphene quantum dot can be specifically focused on the merits of fast hot carrier extraction, tunable properties, and long-term stability [29–32]. Especially, heteroatom doped graphene quantum dots exhibit tunable semiconductive properties and high carrier mobility, processing potential usage in optoelectric devices [29,33]. In fact, the application of graphene quantum dots in PSCs has been reported before [34]. For n-i-p structure, graphene quantum dots modified electron transport layer (ETL) not only suppressed charge recombination but also facilitate the charge transportation at interface between PVK and ETL [35–38]. Flexible PSCs exhibit superior performance and mechanical durability after modification of graphene quantum dots [39]. Doping elements and functional groups into graphene quantum dots through functionalization is also widely used in PSCs [36,40]. Functionalized graphene quantum dots can reduce internal non-radiative recombination through interaction with PVK, improving the efficiency and stability of PSCs [41–43]. However, the design and application of doped graphene quantum dots for Sn-Pb based PSC is rare, which is therefore imperative to investigate.

Here, fine-tuned p-type graphene quantum dots are achieved by doping functional N and Cl elements and used at the interface of PEDOT:PSS/Sn-Pb perovskite. The mismatched energy level of PEDOT:PSS is successfully tuned by N and N, Cl co-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs, and N,Cl-GQDs), increasing the surface work function of PEDOT:PSS to align with FA0.6MA0.4Sn0.6Pb0.4I3 perovskite. Among three designed quantum dots, the N,Cl-GQDs exhibit the best alignment because of the enhanced p-type doping by Cl. Besides, density functional theory (DFT) calculation indicates N,Cl-GQDs reform charge distribution for efficient interface charge transfer. Experimental results also show the N,Cl-GQDs best promote the charge extraction and passivate the defect states in Sn-Pb device by functional N and Cl. As a result, solar cells with N,Cl-GQDs modification achieve the highest PCE with impressive V oc of 0.89 V, FF of 80.4%, and best stability. To the best of our knowledge, the high V oc and FF are among the best reported Sn-Pb works (Summary report in Table. S1 and Fig. S1), suggesting efficient voltage output and charge transport.

Three types of graphene quantum dots (GQDs, N-GQDs, and N,Cl-GQDs) were prepared by one-step hydrothermal method shown in Fig. S2. The size distribution of the graphene quantum dots is 3–7 nm with a well-defined carbon lattice [44,45]. The schema of the graphene quantum dots structure is indicated in Fig. 1a–c. The as fabricated GQDs (Fig. 1a) show a conjugated carbon backbone with hydroxyl at the edge. GQDs is reported to be p-doped material because oxygen atoms are more electronegative than carbon atoms [46]. Differed from GQDs structure, -NH2 is induced at the edge of graphene quantum dot in N-GQDs (N-doped, Fig. 1b). While in N,Cl-GQDs (N and Cl co-doped, Fig. 1c), N is further doped in conjugated carbon backbone as pyrrolic N and Cl exists at the edge of the graphene quantum dots. Doping functional elements in graphene quantum dot lattice structure are believed to restructure the energy states in the band-gap and change the optoelectric properties [33]. To investigate the optical properties of the graphene quantum dots, UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence (PL) behavior of the three samples were recorded. Fig. 1d shows the emission spectra of the three graphene quantum dots excited at 320 nm monochromatic light. GQDs, N-GQDs and N,Cl-GQDs show specific emission peaks near 410 nm, 470 nm and 460 nm, respectively. In addition, the inset of Fig. 1d reveals that the different graphene quantum dots solutions exhibit bright blue fluorescence under 365 nm irradiation, indicating the excellent photoluminescence property. From the emission peak position, the optic energy bandgaps of GQDs, N-GQDs and N,Cl-GQDs are estimated to be 2.95, 2.6 and 2.66 eV respectively. The ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) absorbance spectra and the Tauc plots for graphene quantum dots are shown in Fig. S3. The estimated optical bandgaps of N,Cl-GQDs, N-GQDs, and GQDs are consistent with the values determined by emission spectra.

Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS, Fig. 1e) is utilized to characterize the work function (WF) and valence band maximum (VBM) positions of the three graphene quantum dots. Determining from the photoemission cutoff spectra in Fig. 1e, the WF of GQDs is 4.04 eV, while the WFs of N-GQDs and N, Cl-GQDs are increased to 4.25 and 4.42 eV, respectively. The VBM of GQDs, N-GQDs and N,Cl-GQDs are estimated from the onset energy in Fig. 1e to be −5.05, −5.23 and −5.34 eV, respectively. Considering the above determined optical bandgap, the energy structure of the dots is shown in Fig. 1f. It is noticed that all the graphene quantum dots exhibit p-type character as the Fermi energy levels (EF=-WF) locate more close to the VBM. Obviously, the p-type character is fine-tuned by the N/Cl doping: -NH2 groups at the edge of N-GQDs together with –OH, totally enhance the p-type property as the down-shifted Fermi energy level to valence band edge. According to previous reports, the electron-rich atom as an n-type donor in the graphene quantum dots, while the electron-deficient atom acts is a p-type dopant [47,48]. Obviously, Cl as an electron-withdrawing moiety in N, Cl-GQDs further enhances the p-type character as Fermi energy level moves more negatively and closer to the VBM compared with N-GQDs [31,49,50].

Because the tuned p character (energy level) of graphene quantum dots, the three quantum dot samples are decorated on PEDOT:PSS electrode to tune its surface chemical and electric properties. As is shown in Fig. 2a, the graphene quantum dots were dispersed in isopropanol with 1.5 mg/ml and thinly coated on the PEDOT:PSS layer via a spin-coating method. The resulted in three modified samples are noted as PEDOT:PSS/GQDs (w GQDs), PEDOT:PSS/N-GQDs (w N-GQDs) and PEDOT:PSS/N,Cl-GQDs (w N,Cl-GQDs), while the original PEDOT:PSS is marked as control. The graphene quantum dots treatment totally improved the hydrophobicity of the PEDOT:PSS layer because of the conjugated carbon backbone (Fig. S4). The hydrophilic angle of the surface increases from 14.5° (control) to 24.5° (w GQDs), 49.7° (w N-GQDs), and 37.6° (w N,Cl-GQDs), respectively. It was difficult to observe the graphene quantum dots on PEDOT:PSS surface through the top-view scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image in Fig. S5, owing to very thin layers of graphene quantum dots. AFM surface morphologies (Fig. 2b) indicate graphene quantum dots slightly change the surface roughness factor without obviously changing the surface morphologies, among which the PEDOT:PSS/N,Cl-GQDs exhibits the smoothest surface. The transmission spectra of PEDOT:PSS/FTO (Fig. S6) hardly changes after modification by graphene quantum dots. In XPS of graphene quantum dots modified PEDOT:PSS, the binding energies of S 1 s in graphene quantum dots modified PEDOT:PSS differ from the pure PEDOT:PSS (Fig. S7), indicating the graphene quantum dots with polar groups like -OH -NH2 and -Cl chemically interact with the PEDOT:PSS surface.

It has been reported that electron donor or acceptor molecules can change the work function and energy level positions of the pristine film, even different solvents have an effect on their energy levels [51]. The WF of the PEDOT:PSS films by graphene quantum dots treatment is investigated by Kelvin probe force microscopy (KPFM) measurements (Fig. 2c). The average surface potential of PEDOT:PSS/N-GQDs and PEDOT:PSS/GQDs films increases, which means that the surface WF is up-shifted compared with pure PEDOT:PSS. By contrast, the average surface potential of the PEDOT:PSS/N,Cl-GQDs film decreased by 0.23 V compared to the PEDOT:PSS films, indicating the increased WF of the PEDOT:PSS/N, Cl-GQDs film in respect to PEDOT:PSS.

UPS measurements are further conducted to confirm the exact energy levels of PEDOT:PSS film without and with GQDs modification. As shown in Fig. S8, Compared with the PEDOT:PSS film with a WF of 4.31 eV, the WF of the three modified PEDOT:PSS are 4.45 eV (w N,Cl-GQDs), 4.22 eV (w N-GQDs) and 4.19 eV (w GQDs), consisting with the WF variation in KPFM measurement above. Moreover, the valence band maximum (VBM) are calculated to be − 5.19 eV (control), −5.37 eV (w N, Cl-GQDs), −5.26 eV (w N-GQDs) and −5.15 eV (w GQDs). Thus the energy level diagram of the device is depicted in Fig. 2d. The energy level of HTL changes in accordance with the energy structure of graphene quantum dots because of the surface interaction. PEDOT:PSS/GQDs have higher VBM and Fermi level than PEDOT:PSS, whereas PEDOT:PSS/N-GQDs and PEDOT:PSS/N,Cl-GQDs shows deeper VBM. Especially after the N,Cl-GQDs modification, the VBM (−5.37 eV) of HTL best aligns to the VBM (−5.4 eV) of PVK, and the energy level is the most matched in Fig. 2d. By fine-tune graphene quantum dots with a suitable energy level as the modification layer, the interface energy level mismatch between the transport layer and the Sn-Pb perovskite is successfully controlled.

Conductive atomic force microscopy (C-AFM) as a high-sensitive characterization technique is used to probe charge transport properties of graphene quantum dots decorated HTL (Fig. 2e). Owning to good charge transport property of graphene quantum dots, the modified PEDOT:PSS all exhibit improved charge transport property compared with pure PEDOT:PSS. Of all these samples, the N,Cl-GQDs exhibit the most uniform and highest current distribution in Fig. 2e, indicating the high quality of N,Cl-GQDs based HTL for charge transfer. Besides, the measured direct current (DC) conductivities (σ0) of PEDOT:PSS with or without graphene quantum dot modification consist with the C-AFM. From dark J−V curves of the devices based on the configuration of FTO/PEDOT:PSS/Ag (Fig. 2f), the DC conductivities of the PEDOT:PSS layer modified by the three graphene quantum dots are calculated to be 2.71 mS/cm (w GQDs), 2.65 mS/cm (w N-GQDs), and 3.08 mS/cm (w N,Cl-GQDs), higher than that of the unmodified PEDOT:PSS layer (1.9 mS/cm). In alignment with C-AFM results, PEDOT:PSS modified by doped graphene quantum dots exhibits enhanced conductivities, during which N,Cl-GQDs/PEDOT:PSS best promote the conductivity. The enhanced conductivity of the quantum dots modified PEDOT:PSS is assigned to the π-conjugation of the graphene structure, which would help to reduce the serial resistance of the solar cells.

The interface energy structure change by graphene quantum dots modification is quite evident as shown in the above experiment results. As for the function of graphene quantum dots on the charge transport behaviors of graphene quantum dot/Sn-Pb perovskite (PVK) heterojunction, it is firstly explored by electronic structures calculated by density function theory (DFT). An I vacancy (VI) defect state at SnI6 octahedral is considered to show defect related charge distribution on Sn-Pb PVK. Fig. 3a–h demonstrates conduction band minimum (CBM) and VBM associated charge density distributions, where the graphene quantum dots/Sn-Pb PVK with VI clearly manifests less localized distribution of charges both at CBM (Fig. 3b–d) and VBM (Fig. 3f–h) compared with control Sn-Pb PVK with VI (Fig. 3a and e) [52]. The delocalization of charges benefits both electron and hole transport through the PVK lattice. It is quite contrastive that the CBM-associated charges all distribute throughout the graphene quantum dot plates (Fig. 3b–d) without the presence in PVK structure, ascribing to the existence of the π-conjugated effect of graphene on PVK surface [53]. The π-conjugated effect gives rise to delocalized distribution of charges on the PVK. In terms of the VBM, the N,Cl-GQDs outstandingly delocalize the inner charge distribution in PVK compared to other quantum dots modified cases and change the charge distribution mainly at the PVK surface (Fig. 3f). Such a situation is believed to promote charge transfer from PVK to interface contacting with graphene quantum dots. Compared with N-GQDs (Fig. 3g) and GQDs (Fig. 3h), the N,Cl-GQDs with strong electron negativity Cl at the graphene plate edge is supposed to powerfully change the VBM charge distribution (Fig. 3f). Therefore, the DFT calculations suggest that PVK with N,Cl-GQDs contacting might exhibit the best charge transport property due to the graphene structure and functional doping elements.

To experimentally investigate the charge dynamic process of three graphene quantum dots at the interface of PEDOT:PSS/FA0.6MA0.4Sn0.6Pb0.4I3, steady state photoluminescence (PL) and time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) were characterized for PVK films fabricated on FTO/PEDOT:PSS with or without modification. Firstly, the light absorption of PVK films on PEDOT:PSS with or without modification layer is similar as observed in UV-Vis absorbance spectra (Fig. S9a). The optical bandgaps of FA0.6MA0.4Sn0.6Pb0.4I3 PVK with or without modified layer are estimated to be ~1.25 eV (Fig. S9b), consistent with that reported in the literature [54,55]. Secondly, Sn-Pb PVK on N-GQDs and N,Cl-GQDs modified PEDOT:PSS exhibit weakened PL intensity in PL spectra (Fig. 3i), arising from the boosted charge transfer across the interfaces of PVK/N-GQDs/PEDOT:PSS and PVK/N,Cl-GQDs/PEDOT:PSS. The TRPL in Fig. 3j shows the same trend with steady-state PL and the decay fitting life of PVK is shown in Table S2, where the fastest decay is observed in PEDOT:PSS/N,Cl-GQDs/PVK sample. Better energy level alignment and the special electric structure of N,Cl-GQDs for charge transport greatly benefit the hole extraction from Sn-Pb PVK to modified PEDOT:PSS.

During the structure optimization in DFT calculation, the chemical interaction of N,Cl-GQDs with the under-coordinated Sn is noticed. As shown in Fig. S10, we noticed Cl on N,Cl-GQDs moves forward to VI after structure optimization, indicating the additional interaction of Sn and Cl. These chemical interactions induced by Cl and N in graphene quantum dots are supposed to passivate the defect states in Sn-Pb PVK as indicated in Fig. 4a,b. Under-coordinate Sn/Pb, VSn, VPb and VI are easily formed by Sn-I and Pb-I bonds breaking, resulting in serious carrier nonradiative recombination [56]. Effective defect passivation strategies have been investigated in previous studies. Utilizing Lewis acid-base and halide ions can effectively heal defect states by new chemical coordination/bonding [57]. Here, the function of –NH2 and Cl on graphene quantum dots to chemical interact with Sn/Pb sites in PVK is investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS). In Fig. 4 c, it is observed that the binding energy of Pb 4f shifts most obviously for N-GQDs modified PbI2/SnI2 films (0.24 eV) in comparison with other quantum dots modified and pristine PbI2/SnI2. The phenomenon can be attributed to the coordination between Pb2+ and the electron-rich N atom in N-GQDs, which can passivate the defect states of under-coordinated Pb (Fig. 4a). N-GQDs show a strong chemical interaction with Pb 4 f, whereas N,Cl-GQDs indicate the obvious change in Sn 3d as shown in Fig. 4d. It is quite interesting to observe the Sn 3d core level of N,Cl-GQDs modified PbI2/SnI2 shows the largest shift up by 0.13 eV compared to other samples. Cl with high electronegativity effectively changes the Sn binding energy. It is concluded that N,Cl-GQDs act at Sn-Pb PVK surface mainly by Sn-Cl bonding as shown in Fig. 4b. Stronger bonding of Sn-Cl than Sn-I definitely benefits from inhibiting the formation of VSn and VI. From the XPS characterization, the N-GQDs and N,Cl-GQDs are supposed to passivate the interface defect states. Especially the Cl in N,Cl-GQDs function toward the chemical active Sn site, thus benefiting for the less defective interface of PEDOT:PSS/N,Cl-GQDs/Sn-Pb PVK.

Besides the energy band alignment and defect passivation effect, N-GQDs and N,Cl-GQDs on PEDOT:PSS is considered to affect the PVK film formation by the hydrophobic property and active N/Cl sites. Top-view SEM images of Sn-Pb PVK films grown on PEDOT:PSS with and without surface modification are shown in Fig. 4e. It can be seen that the N,Cl-GQDs and N-GQDs interlayers do benefit the growth of perovskite films and lead to an enlarged grain size and fewer grain boundaries. Hydrophobic nature of graphene quantum dots at the interface reduces the nuclei density of Sn-Pb PVK, while N and Cl functional sites encourage homogeneous nucleation. Thus both promote high quality PVK film with large grain size [58–61]. Larger grain size can significantly reduce the grain boundaries and effectively inhibit the charge recombination between the grain boundaries. However, the PVK grown on PEDOT:PSS/GQDs is slightly smaller than the control samples. X-ray diffraction (XRD) of perovskite films in Fig. S11 consists of the SEM results, showing increased crystallinity of perovskite films on N,Cl-GQDs and N-GQDs modified PEDOT:PSS.

The trap densities of the control and modified films were evaluated by SCLC analysis to further explore the defect passivation effectiveness of the three graphene quantum dots on Sn-Pb PVK. The dark current-voltage (I-V) curves of hole-only devices with the structure of FTO/PEDOT:PSS/Sn-Pb PVK/Spiro-OMeTAD/Ag are shown in Fig. 4f. The trap density can be deduced from the trap-filled limit voltage (VTFL), which is corresponding to the onset voltage of the trap-filled limit region [62,63]. The trap density of the PVK on PEDOT:PSS is 1.73 × 1016 cm−3. By contrast, the trap densities of PVK on PEDOT:PSS with N-GQDs and N,Cl-GQDs are significantly reduced to 1.57 × 1016 and 1.35 × 1016 cm−3 respectively, illustrating the N,Cl-GQDs and N-GQDs with good control of N and Cl distribution is more efficient for eliminating trap states in Sn-Pb perovskite.

The TRPL plots of FA0.6MA0.4Sn0.6Pb0.4I3/graphene quantum dots on glass is investigated to explore the trap states of Sn-Pb PVK with interface modification (Fig. 4g), which is consistent with steady-state PL (Fig. S12). The calculated PL lifetime of pure Sn-Pb PVK on glass is 42.44 ns (Table S3). It is worth noting that for control samples grown on glass, the PVK lifetime with and without the PEDOT:PSS is not much different and even lower in the FTO/PVK compared with FTO/PEDOT:PSS/PVK. We speculate that it is caused by the poorer quality of PVK films grown on glass without HTL. As shown in Fig. S13, it can be observed that the perovskite film deposited on the glass has uneven grain size and poor film formation compared to the film deposited on FTO/PEDOT:PSS. In addition, poor film-forming quality is more likely to cause samples to decompose during shipping and testing. These will lead to a decrease in the fluorescence lifetime of PVK. However, we found that the fluorescence lifetime of PVK grown on N,Cl-GQDs reached more than 90 ns, which is almost twice that of other samples. Combined with the enhancement of crystallinity and trap states passivation by N,Cl-GQDs, it demonstrates the charge recombination of Sn-Pb PVK films is successfully suppressed by N,Cl-GQDs. We also observed that the lifetime of PVK grown on GQDs alone is significantly reduced. Based on the energy level of GQDs and the deterioration of the PVK crystallinity on GQDs, the reduced PL intensity and PL lifetime are ascribed to the charge recombination in PVK/GQDs film.

PSCs with an inverted structure of FTO/PEDOT:PSS/FA0.6MA0.4Sn0.6Pb0.4I3/PCBM/BCP/Ag were fabricated ( Fig. 5a). Due to the high quality Sn-Pb PVK on N,Cl-GQDs/PEDOT:PSS, the cross-section picture of the modified device (Fig. 5 c) exhibits larger grains and uniform film compared with the control device (Fig. 5 b). Primary performance test (Fig. S14 and Table. S4) indicates N,Cl-GQDs incorporated device achieves the highest PCE of 19.8% with considerably improved V oc of 0.830 V, J sc= 30.8 mA/cm2, and FF= 77.6%, much better than the control device of 17.8%, with J sc of 30.1 mA/cm2, a low V oc= 0.786 V, and FF= 75.2%. Notably, the energy loss is successfully suppressed to 0.42 eV in N,Cl-GQDs modified PSC, compared with ~0.46 eV of the control sample, assigning to the best band alignment and reduced defects in w N,Cl-GQDs device. To realize more efficient device, strategy of supine surface and grain boundary passivation is applied to eliminate the deep trap states [64]. Ethylenediamine (EDA) is modified at the perovskite surface followed by the reference [65]. By applying PEDOT:PSS/ FA0.6MA0.4Sn0.6Pb0.4I3/EDA structure with evaporated C60/BCP/Ag, enhanced V oc and PCE are obtained for all the devices, indicating the high quality of our devices (Fig. 5d). The control device reaches PCE of 19.7%, with J sc of 30.0 mA/cm2, V oc= 0.861 V and FF= 76.1% (Table. S5). N,Cl-GQDs PSC exhibits outstanding device performance of 21.5%, with V oc= 0.886 V, J sc= 30.3 mA/cm2 and FF= 80.4%. Such a low energy loss (~0.36 eV) and high FF is among the best reported Sn-Pb works (Summary report in Table. S1 and Fig. S1), suggesting efficient voltage output and charge transport. N-GQDs moderately increase the PCE to 20.2%, with V oc= 0.881 V, J sc= 30.1 mA/cm2, and FF= 76.3%. However, incorporation of GQDs decreases the PCE to 18.6%, with reduced J
sc = 29.9 mA/cm2, FF= 72.2% and V oc= 0.862 V. The declined J sc and FF are due to the unmatched energy level of PEDOT:PSS/GQDs/Sn-Pb PVK interface to block charge transport and enhance recombination.

In Fig. 5e, we have the statistical performance of 20 devices, where the distribution of the PCE data further illustrates the reliability and reproducibility of our devices. From the external quantum efficiency (EQE) spectrum in Fig. 5f, J sc is integrated to be 29.5 (control), 30.1 (w N,Cl-GQDs), 29.6 (w N-GQDs) and 28.9 mA/cm2 (w GQDs), which are close to the values as observed in J–V curves.

We carried out the dark current analysis to study the leakage current formed by carrier recombination in the solar cells (Fig. 5g). The suppressed current in the dark of device w N,Cl-GQDs indicates the decrease of leakage current and better defect passivation [66]. Therefore, a larger V
oc is obtained in the corresponding solar cell. Additionally, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and transient photo-voltage decay (TPV) (Figs. S15, S16) are used to check the recombination at the HTM/perovskite interface [67]. The enhanced diameter of the arc in Nyquist plots and the prolonged decay time TPV spectra proved that the devices modified by N, Cl-GQDs and N-GQDs can effectively reduce the recombination loss and increase the device photovoltage. In addition, the series resistance (Rs) is obtained according to the equivalent circuit fitting. As shown in Table R6, the Rs of the device modified with N,Cl-GQDs and N-GQDs is reduced, indicating the improved charge transport at the interface [68].

The transient photo-current decay (TPC) method is performed to investigate the charge transport property of the solar cell devices [67]. TPC response in Fig. 5h reduces from 14 µs of control PSC to 7.4 and 11 µs of PEDOT:PSS/N,Cl-GQDs and PEDOT:PSS/N-GQDs based device respectively, proving that carrier extraction and transmission is the fastest in PEDOT:PSS/N,Cl-GQDs based PSC. This is due to the best energy level arrangement between HTL and PVK and the efficient charge extraction by quantum dots.

At last, Fig. 5i shows the shelf life of PSCs with and without graphene quantum dots (stored in a N2 filled glovebox). The PSCs with N,Cl-GQDs retain 90% of the initial PCE after 1000 h, much better than the control device and other graphene quantum dots modified samples. It is due to the reduced defect states of PVK film and the reformative chemical environment of PEDOT:PSS surface by N,Cl-GQDs modification.

In summary, three chemically doped graphene quantum dots are applied at PEDOT:PSS/Sn-Pb perovskite interface. Due to the heteroatom doping, the energy band structure is tuned by N and Cl elements. Among three quantum dots, the N,Cl-GQDs exhibit the enhanced p-type doping, thereby inducing the best band energy alignment between PEDOT:PSS/N,Cl-GQDs and PVK. Besides, the DFT calculation shows that N,Cl-GQDs reformate the interface charge distribution for efficient charge transport. Experiments also indicate the charge extraction and defect passivation are promoted by N,Cl-GQDs. The best optoelectronic property of PEDOT:PSS/N,Cl-GQDs/PVK is assigned to the graphitic π-conjugated charge transport and electronegative Cl, contributing to the efficient charge transport and reduction of trap states. The optimized Sn-Pb PSCs with N,Cl-GQDs show the highest efficiency with the lowest energy loss. Due to the reduced defect states in the device and the modified surface of PEDOT:PSS, the shelf stability of PSC with N,Cl-GQDs is the highest, which keeps 90% after 1000 h. To further enhance the PCE and stability of the LB Sn-Pb based PSCs, the anti-oxidation of Sn2+ is being carried out in our future work.

ce:section.ce:para: J.Z. conceived the idea and supervised the project. J.Z. and T.G. designed the experiment. T.G. performed the perovskite synthesis, most of the characterizations (SEM, UV–vis, FTIR, XPS, UPS, etc.), and device fabrication/testing. D.C. provides analysis of graphene quantum dots and their properties. W.X. and W.H. contributed to the theoretical simulations. C.W, H.W. and T.M. performed the EQE and I-V characterization and the specimen preparation. Z.D and Z.Z. assisted in the PL, AFM, and KPFM measurements and analysis. All the authors contributed to the discussion on the material and device characteristics. T.G. and J.Z. prepared the paper with contributions from all the other co-authors.
